content.problemStatement: You are given a sequence **a1, a2, ..., aN**. Count the number of triples (**i, j, k**) such that 1 ≤ **i** < **j** < **k** ≤ **N** and GCD(**ai, aj, ak**) = 1. Here **GCD** stands for the Greatest Common Divisor.
content.inputFormat: First line contains T, the number of test cases. Each test case contains a single integer N on the first line, followed by N space-separated integers on the second line.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the number of triples (i, j, k) such that 1 ≤ i < j < k ≤ N and GCD(ai, aj, ak) = 1.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Any triple will be a coprime one.

solution.approach: The key insight is to use the concept of GCD to find coprime triples. We can calculate the GCD of three numbers by finding the GCD of the first two numbers and then finding the GCD of the result and the third number. We iterate over all possible triples (i, j, k) and check if the GCD of ai, aj, and ak is 1. If it is, we increment the count of coprime triples.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N^3) per test case, O(T * N^3) total. No precomputation is done.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) — the dominant structure is the array to store the input integers.
